data.title: European Midday Briefing: Surge in Gilt Yields Continues, Raising Concern, as Sterling Falls
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Stocks:

European indices were mixed, with the FTSE 100 posting solid gains after sterling hit a one-year low against the dollar following a sharp selloff in U.K. government bonds.

Elsewhere European shipping companies were under pressure after a strike was averted at major U.S. ports, with Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd both down more than 5%.

Jefferies said freight rates have been boosted recently as retailers increase their stockpile of inventory amid uncertainty surrounding tariffs and the potential port strikes.

"The supply/demand balance remains steady, but a soft patch could come later this year."

Stocks to Watch

The impact of lower interest rates sees a continuation of the challenging operating environment for Nordic banks, Jefferies said separately, as it lowered its EPS estimates by 1%-3% on average across the sector.

"On the macro front, economic sentiment remains weak, and we await an economic recovery in Sweden."

Nordea remains its only buy-rated stock among the Nordic banks, as it continues to see better value elsewhere in the European banking context.

U.S. Markets:

The U.S. stock market will be closed and the bond market will shut early, but Thursday will bring a plethora of timely commentary from Federal Reserve officials as the central bank faces an uncertain inflation outlook.

The Fed's Collins and Harker will both speak around 14:00 GMT, with Barkin, Bowman and Schmid delivering remarks at events later in the day.

The next big impact on rate expectations will be Friday's nonfarm payrolls data.

Forex:

The dollar is likely to remain firm after the Fed minutes confirmed its more cautious stance about cutting interest rates, ING said, adding that the currency is likely to stay strong into Trump's inauguration on January 20.

Sterling could extend its losses--falling below $1.2--on growing investor unease over the ongoing selloff in gilts, MUFG said.

The rise in gilt yields appears broadly in line with similar moves in other bond markets, but rising borrowing costs could put pressure on the government to tighten fiscal policy.

Higher yields due to the Bank of England's caution over cutting interest rates boosted sterling last year.

"Recent price action highlights though, that higher yields are not always positive for a currency if they are driven by unease over the public finances and inflation in the U.K."

Pepperstone said a falling currency and that rising yields were a classic sign of fiscal de-anchoring and of participants losing confidence in the government's ability to exert control over the fiscal backdrop.

It added that it wasn't the same situation as when the 2022 tax-slashing budget under former Prime Minister Liz Truss sparked market turmoil but "things are clearly on very shaky ground" and selling sterling remains its preference.

UOB said the pound is likely to continue its downtrend against the dollar, in coming weeks according to the weekly chart .

Bonds:

Issuance in the eurozone so far has been strongly received, but bond yields keep rising.

"For most of December it was U.S. Treasuries that led the way, since the holidays it has been eurozone government bonds and since yesterday it is U.K. gilts," Commerzbank said.

The rise in Treasury yields in recent weeks has been driven by rising inflation expectations, the repricing of the Fed's interest rates and higher term premium, Metzler said.

"While a rise in the term premium is likely to continue (political uncertainty is likely to remain high under Trump), the mood regarding the Fed, the economy and inflation could quickly change again."

The first signs of that came Wednesday when the Fed's Waller said he expected milder inflation and more rate cuts.

Gilt yields continued to rise--more than other bond yields--and if the current moves continue, it could potentially be evidence of a buyer's strike or capital flight, according to Fidelity.

Energy:

Oil was lower due to a stronger dollar and rise in U.S. fuel stockpiles.

"From a technical perspective, crude oil [WTI] is now in the medium-term bullish consolidation zone and should remain there above the $72.85pb support," Swissquote Bank said.

Prices had a strong start to 2025, supported by colder temperatures boosting heating fuel consumption and worries over Iranian and Russian supplies.

Still, broader concerns of an oversupplied market this year and uncertainties over Chinese growth limit the upside.

Metals:

Gold edged higher despite a stronger dollar, as investors seek safe-haven assets amid growing market uncertainties.

A weaker-than-expected report on U.S. private employment for December provided some reassurance that the Fed might be less cautious about lowering rates.

But the minutes released on Wednesday suggested officials will hold rates steady for now, revealing they saw risks of higher-than-expected inflation partly due to Trump's potential future policies.

"That should hardly come as a surprise given that the median interest rate projection now shows only 50bp in cuts this year," Capital Economics said.

Global Lithium Outlook

Mergers and acquisitions are likely to remain prevalent in the global lithium industry , as valuations decline on expectations for lower lithium prices in the medium term, Goldman Sachs said.

It said the lithium market should be soft again this year as a number of projects ramp up, and reckon producers will be focused on cost-cutting and production efficiency.

EMEA HEADLINES

German Industrial Production Rises Despite Fears of Layoffs, Tariffs

German industrial production ticked up in November last year, offering a glimmer of hope for the beleaguered sector in the face of sweeping job cuts and the threat of import tariffs from the incoming Trump administration.

Output rose 1.5% on month in November, German statistics agency Destatis said Thursday, better than the 0.5% rise expected by economists in a poll compiled by The Wall Street Journal.

China Blasts EU's Foreign Subsidies Law as Unfair Trade Barrier

Beijing has called out the European Union for imposing what it says are unfair trade barriers on Chinese companies, ending a monthslong investigation into the bloc's efforts to protect its businesses from foreign subsidies.

China's commerce ministry released the results of its probe into the EU's Foreign Subsidies Regulation on Thursday, saying that European authorities have implemented the rule selectively in a way that infringes on Chinese companies' interests.

Tesco Sticks to Profit Target After Posting Christmas Sales Growth

Tesco reiterated its profit target for its fiscal year after reporting sales growth during the Christmas period.

The U.K. supermarket chain on Thursday said it continues to expect retail adjusted operating profit-its preferred metric, which strips out exceptional and other one-off items-in the fiscal year ending in February of around 2.9 billion pounds ($3.59 billion).

Marks & Spencer Focused on Growth Plan After Christmas Sales Boost Results

Marks & Spencer said it is confident in making progress in the remainder of the year after it recorded a positive performance during the key Christmas season and as it continues with its growth plan.

The U.K. retailer said Thursday that for the 13 weeks to Dec. 28 it made sales of 4.06 billion pounds ($5.02 billion), up 5.6% compared with the year-earlier period. The result came ahead of analysts' projections of 3.96 billion pounds, according to a Visible Alpha poll of estimates.

GLOBAL NEWS

Trump's Day 1: Incoming president looks set to act on tariffs, crypto, energy and immigration

On his first day back in office, President-elect Donald Trump is planning to go big on executive orders and other actions, including ones that aim to roll back some of his predecessor's policies. It'll be similar to how President Joe Biden rolled out a raft of orders on the day of his inauguration four years ago and targeted a number of Trump 1.0 initiatives.

So which upcoming Trump executive actions on Jan. 20 could have an effect on your investing portfolio or your wallet? Here's a brief guide.

Musk Says DOGE's Goal to Cut $2 Trillion in Spending Is 'Best-Case Outcome'

Elon Musk reiterated that his Department of Government Efficiency will try to cut $2 trillion from federal spending, but he said they may not reach that goal.

"I think if we try for $2 trillion, we've got a good shot at getting one, " Musk said Wednesday in a conversation with Mark Penn, chairman and chief executive of ad company Stagwell, that was livestreamed on Musk's social-media platform X.

Trump's NATO Vision Spells Trouble for the Alliance

President-elect Donald Trump's latest demands of America's NATO partners-that they cede territory to the U.S. and spend more on defense than Washington itself does-risk undermining allies' confidence and potentially emboldening adversaries.

In a press conference Tuesday, Trump raised the prospect of forcibly taking over Canada and Greenland, which is part of Denmark. Canada and Denmark are founding members of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, and the U.S. is treaty-bound to protect them.

Israel's Defense Industry Booms on Foreign Demand and War at Home

TEL AVIV-After a year marked by war and calls for isolating Israel on the world stage, its defense industry is on track to sell more weapons than ever in its history, spurred by foreign demand for its aerial-defense systems and significant government spending aimed at funding Israel's ongoing military campaigns.

The surge in weapons sales for Israel's top three defense companies-among the world's top 10 defense exporters-comes amid widespread arms embargoes on Israel, from even some of its allies. Other countries have suspended their acquisition of Israeli arms over the war in Gaza.

Los Angeles Wildfires Leave at Least Five Dead as Uncontained Blazes Intensify LOS ANGELES-Portions of the heavily populated Hollywood neighborhood were ordered to evacuate Wednesday evening as a new fire broke out in the hills above the center of this city battling numerous deadly blazes.

At least five people were dead and tens of thousands were forced to evacuate as six wildfires simultaneously hit the most populated county in America. More than 2,000 structures were damaged or destroyed, 123,000 people lost power, and the region's firefighting resources were stretched to their limits.

Write to clare.kinloch@wsj.com

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.

data.title: Canadian Natural Resources forecasts higher production in 2025
data.description:
Canadian Natural Resources CNQ said on Tuesday it expects production to increase 12% in 2025, betting on higher demand amid tight supplies.

The company said annual average production in 2025 is targeted to be between 1.51 million and 1.555 million barrels of oil equivalent per day (boepd), resulting in production growth of approximately 170,000 boepd or 12% over 2024 levels based on the mid-point of forecast.

data.title: Acer Announces Preliminary Full Year 2024 Revenues at NT$264.71B, Up 9.7% >2353.TW
Air Products and Chemicals Pours Cold Water on Activist Investor Mantle Ridge's CEO, Chair Nominations
data.description:
Air Products and Chemicals APD said late Wednesday that its shareholder Mantle Ridge's proposed nominations of candidates for the chief executive officer and chair roles "should be troubling" to stockholders.

The company's statement is its board's response to activist investor Mantle Ridge's shareholder letter introducing four director nominees, including Executive Chair Dennis Reilley and its proposed CEO candidate, Eduardo Menezes.

Reilley has not held a management position in 18 years and would be coming into a company and industry that have undergone tremendous change in the last two decades, rendering any "experience" that he has as stale, Air Products said.

"Menezes, who has never been a public company CEO, only had responsibility for one region when he worked at Linde, and, as acknowledged by Mantle Ridge, was passed over for CEO at Linde," Air Products added.

Mantle Ridge did not immediately respond to MTNewswires' request for comment early on Thursday.

Air Products' shares slipped 0.4% at the close on Wednesday.

In its shareholder letter, Mantle Ridge urged investors to vote for its nominees "to enhance Air Products' performance and create the long-term value that shareholders deserve." Mantle Ridge said it beneficially owns $1.3 billion of Air Products' outstanding common shares.

data.title: Nordic forward prices fall on wetter weather view, higher water reserves
data.description:
Nordic forward power prices fell on Thursday, as forecasts for the current week showed wetter and warmer weather in the hydropower-reliant region, with higher water reserves also adding pressure.

* Nordic front-quarter baseload power contract (ENOFBLQc1) fell 0.75 euros to 17.3 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) at 1015 GMT.

* Nordic front-year (ENOFBLYc1) lost 0.3 euros to 32.5 euros per MWh.

* "The downtrend could very well continue today where the forecasts are even wetter and milder than yesterday," analysts at Energi Danmark said in a note.

* "The hydro balance surplus should grow if the forecasts turn out to be true and the outlook is therefore still bearish for the Nordic power market."

* Nordic water reserves available 15 days ahead (PCAEC00) were seen at 23.84 terawatt hours (TWh) above normal, compared with 22.87 TWh above normal on Wednesday.

* In the European carbon market, the benchmark contract (CFI2Zc1) was down 0.22 euro at 71.9 euros a metric ton.

* German year-ahead power (TRDEBYc1) slipped 0.3% to 89.1 euros per MWh.

* European prompt power prices were mixed with increased wind supply weighing on the German price, while the French price gained as temperatures were seen falling in the country, increasing demand expectations.

* "The northeast of Central Europe will continue to be partially cloudy with occasional light precipitation mostly as rain," Georg Muller, meteorologist at LSEG, said in a note.

* However, "next week will be generally calm and dry in most areas."

* The Nordic power price for next-day physical delivery (FXSYSAL=NPX), or system price, rose 47.75 euros to 96.88 euros per MWh.

data.title: LG Electronics Forecasts Record 2024 Revenue
data.description:
LG Electronics 066570 forecasts a consolidated revenue of 87.74 trillion won in 2024, marking a new record for the highest annual revenue in the firm's history.

The South Korean electronics products maker also expects its annual operating profit to hit 3.43 trillion won, according to a Wednesday news release.

Over the past four years, LG's revenue grew at a compound annual rate of over 10%, driven by strategic shifts such as subscription services, direct-to-consumer initiatives, and expanded business-to-business operations.

Despite challenges, profitability stayed stable, with positive growth fueled by portfolio realignment, though shipping costs and inventory adjustments impacted second-half profits, the release said.

Shares of LG Electronics fell over 1% at market close on Thursday.

data.description: Attached is a major shareholder announcement from Eyrir Invest hf., in English and Icelandic.

data.title: Ajmera Realty Clocks 7% Increase in Fiscal Q3 Sales Value
data.description:
Ajmera Realty & Infra India AJMERA recorded sales value of 2.70 billion Indian rupees in the fiscal third quarter, up 7% from the year-ago period, according to a press release filed with the Indian bourses on Thursday.

Collections during the quarter ended Dec. 31 rose 10% year on year to 1.67 billion rupees, the release said.

data.title: Alfa Laval to Open Food Innovation Center in Denmark
data.description:
Alfa Laval ALFA said Thursday it will establish a food innovation center at the headquarters of its food and water business in Copenhagen, Denmark.

The 1,200-square-meter facility will serve as a research and development hub to help food producers address the challenges of expanding production while minimizing environmental impact.

Works have already started and the facility is scheduled to open in 2027, according to the Swedish heat transfer, separation and fluid handling products group.

data.title: Nexstim to Deliver Navigated Brain Stimulation System to US Healthcare
data.description:
Nexstim NXTMH secured an order to provide its Navigated Brain Stimulation System 5 with its NexSpeech module to a healthcare provider in the US state of Ohio.

The Finnish medtech company's combined technology will be used by the customer for the preprocedural mapping of the motor cortex of the brain and speech areas, evaluation of surgical risks and neuromonitoring, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: S&P Assigns BBB+ Rating to AmBank's Proposed US Dollar Bonds
data.description:
S&P Global Ratings has placed a BBB+ long-term issue rating on AmBank's proposed US dollar senior unsecured bonds, according to a Thursday release.

The bonds' rating is in line with the Malaysia-based bank's issuer credit rating, reflecting S&P's view that the assets will rank equally with the bank's other obligations.

The bank will channel the proceeds for general working capital purposes.

AmBank is a subsidiary of AMMB Holdings Berhad AMBANK.

data.title: Cosco Capital Repurchases More Shares
data.description:
Cosco Capital COSCO bought back 200,000 shares at between 5.53 Philippine pesos and 5.55 pesos apiece, according to a Wednesday Philippine bourse disclosure.

The retail holding company has so far spent 2.03 billion pesos out of the 4 billion pesos allocated under its buyback program.

data.title: Apple Defends Siri After $95 Million Privacy Lawsuit Settlement
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

Apple sought to allay privacy concerns about its Siri voice assistant days after it agreed to pay $95 million to settle a lawsuit related to the feature, saying it has never sold data collected by Siri or used it for advertising purposes.

The company said in a statement on Wednesday that it had "never used Siri data to build marketing profiles, never made it available for advertising, and never sold it to anyone for any purpose."

Apple said Siri uses on-device processing whenever possible--with information not transmitted to Apple servers in such cases--and that its searches and requests that do require input from servers aren't associated with a user's account.

It added that Siri doesn't retain audio recordings of interactions "unless users explicitly opt in to help improve Siri, and even then, the recordings are used solely for that purpose."

The statement comes a week after Apple agreed to pay $95 million in cash to resolve a class-action lawsuit that alleged the company obtained private communications and shared them with third parties without consent through Siri.

The plaintiffs in the lawsuit alleged that Siri was activated unintentionally and went on to share confidential or private discussions overheard by the voice-driven virtual assistant with Apple.

Apple has denied wrongdoing in the case.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: Goldman Sachs Adjusts Price Target on eBay to $55 From $53, Keeps Sell Rating
data.description: eBay EBAY has an average rating of hold and mean price target of $62.64, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: KKR Extends Deadline for Tender Offer in Fuji Soft Take-Private Proposal
data.description:
KKR KKR said Thursday it has extended its second tender offer to take Japan's Fuji Soft private until Jan. 24.

The 9,451 Japanese yen ($59.78) per share offer for shares of the software company it does not already own was scheduled to expire Thursday.

KKR said the extension is meant to "allow the company's shareholders and share option holders to make a considered decision" on the proposal.

The company also continued to disparage rival Bain Capital's offer to buy 31.4 million shares or 49.9% of the company as a "hostile partial tender offer with the aim of seizing control of the company."

"If Bain Capital, with no outstanding conditions precedent to fulfil, has not launched an unsolicited tender offer as it said it would, it is because Bain Capital either has no actual intention to launch an unsolicited tender offer or that it is unable to do so," the company said.

KKR added that Bain Capital and allied members of Fuji Soft's founding family reached out to through an intermediary in late December to propose working together to take the company private.

Bain Capital and Fuji Soft did not immediately reply to MT Newswires' requests for comment.

data.title: SAP to Finish FY24 Strong Despite Challenges, Baader Helvea Says
data.description:
SAP SAP is expected to meet its revenue growth guidance in 2024, recording a "solid finish" despite challenging conditions, Baader Helvea Equity Research said Wednesday.

The German software company forecasts double-digit growth in cloud and software revenue, cloud revenue, and operating profit in 2024.

Baader Helvea projects a 9% annual growth in SAP's 2024 revenue to 33.95 billion euros, against the Visible Alpha consensus estimate of 33.89 billion euros. It also estimates the group's EPS at 4.42 euros, compared with the consensus of 4.45 euros.

The company will release its full-year results on Jan. 28, with analysts of the view that SAP will confirm its ambition to raise revenue beyond 37.5 billion euros in 2025. Citing the significance of the final quarter for 2024, the research firm is also keeping an eye out for a pre-announcement.

Baader Helvea rated SAP at add, with a price target of 230 euros. "Following the release of the 4Q24 results, we have to roll our valuation from 2025 to 2026, which implies - based on our current estimates - an uplift of our target price from EUR 230 to EUR 265," analysts said.

data.title: Malta Trade Deficit Narrows in November
data.description:
Malta’s trade deficit narrowed to EUR 136.3 million in November 2024 from EUR 186.6 billion in the same month a year earlier, as exports rose while imports declined.

Exports increased 2.2% year-on-year to EUR 509 million, driven by higher sales for miscellaneous transactions and commodities, food, and chemicals.

Meanwhile, imports dropped 5.8% to EUR 645.3 million, mainly due to reduced purchases for machinery and transport equipment and chemicals.

Considering the first eleven months of 2024, the country’s trade gap widened to EUR 4,234.1 million from EUR 3,851.5 million in the corresponding period of the previous year.

data.title: 'Time for a rebound': UBS ups Repsol to 'buy'
data.description:
** UBS upgrades Repsol REP to "buy" from "neutral" saying it sees a rebound for the Spanish oil and gas company's shares on improved macro outlook in 2025

** "Refining margins, the key driver for the shares, have rebounded, and more refinery closures and project delays have been announced, leading to a healthier balance for 2025" - UBS

** It also expects oil prices to be steady and the U.S. gas price to increase, and consequently sees an upside risk to consensus on earnings and shareholder returns for Repsol

** The broker raises its 2025 EPS forecasts for Repsol by 17%, putting it 8% above consensus

** Out of 29 analysts that cover Repsol, 14 rate the stock "strong buy" or "buy",​ 11 "hold" and four "strong sell" or "sell"

** The shares could see their best day since November 2024, rising 2%

** They are among best performers on Spain's Ibex 35 Index IBC

data.title: Market Chatter: Saudi Arabia to Trim Crude Oil Deliveries to China in February
data.description:
Saudi Arabia plans to decrease its crude oil deliveries to China in February, Reuters reported Thursday, citing trade sources.

Saudi Arabian Oil (SASE:2222), d/b/a Saudi Aramco, will reportedly deliver 43.5 million barrels of crude oil next month, compared with 46 million barrels in January.

Saudi Aramco did not immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: U.S. Companies and Investors in Japan Are at Risk From U.S. Steel Backlash — Heard on the Street — WSJ
data.description:
By Jacky Wong

The 1980s economic vibes are back. Japanese stocks scaled new heights in 2024. And the U.S. just stopped a Japanese acquisition of an American industrial icon on national security grounds.

The Biden administration's blocking of Nippon Steel's acquisition of U.S. Steel isn't only misguided. It has also rattled Tokyo and risks straining relations with one of America's closest allies in Asia.

While conditions are superficially reminiscent of the trade frictions of four decades ago, Japan and the U.S. are now deeply enmeshed in each other's economies, with new investment links flourishing even in the past few years. Japanese Prime Minister Shigeru Ishiba's warning about a potential chilling effect on Japanese investment in the U.S. should not be taken lightly.

The insular corporate culture in Japan has long frustrated foreign investors, but that has started changing. Years of governance reforms and pressure from regulators and investors for companies to improve governance and returns have finally started bearing fruit.

The bidding war currently under way for Japanese software developer Fuji Soft between two private-equity giants, Bain Capital and KKR, is a case in point. Even retail giant Seven & i Holdings, which operates the 7-Eleven convenience stores in Japan and the U.S., is in play, with Canada's Alimentation Couche-Tard circling what could be Japan's largest-ever takeover.

The total value of private equity-backed deals in Japan reached a record 5.9 trillion yen, equivalent to $39 billion, in 2023 — twice the amount from the previous year, according to Bain & Co. U.S. equity giants like Bain, Carlyle and KKR have all stepped up their investments in the country.

This makes the U.S. government's intervention particularly poorly timed. The risk now is that protectionism could undermine the progress in opening up Japanese markets as national security is being thrown around as a reason for resisting foreign acquisitions. The next time a U.S. activist investor agitates for the breakup of an underperforming Japanese conglomerate, or an American private-equity giant takes an interest in an acquisition, they are quite likely to encounter a less friendly reception in Japan.

And that also hurts the alliance between the two countries against China. The Biden administration managed to convince Japan to sign up on chip restrictions against China at the expense of its equipment manufacturers like Tokyo Electron. A less trusting relationship between the two countries could hamper such efforts, especially if the incoming Trump administration adopts a similarly protectionist stance toward Japan.

Nations around the world are fretting over Chinese industrial overcapacity, which could flood the world with cheap goods. The merger of Nippon Steel and U.S. Steel, for example, would have strengthened the combined company's ability to compete against Chinese rivals. And Japan also is a key partner with the U.S. in tackling the challenging from China's rising dominance in new emerging sectors like electric vehicles or batteries. That makes the U.S.-Japan cooperation more vital than ever — both sides would do well to remember that their economic relationship is no longer a zero-sum game.

In trying to protect American steel, President Biden risks forging something far more dangerous: cracks in a crucial political and economic alliance.

Write to Jacky Wong at jacky.wong@wsj.com

data.title: TikTok Ban at the Supreme Court: What to Know Ahead of Oral Arguments — WSJ
data.description:
By Jacob Gershman and Sarah E. Needleman

The clock is ticking for TikTok. The Chinese-owned video-sharing platform used by nearly half the country could disappear from U.S. app stores later this month under a new security law. On Friday, TikTok will get the chance to argue its case before the Supreme Court, which is deciding whether the government can ban the platform if it doesn't sever ties with China.

With the app's fate hanging in the balance, here is a look at the case and the potential fallout.

What is the U.S. government's argument for banning TikTok?

The U.S. government has expressed alarm about China's control of TikTok for years, warning that Beijing could exploit the platform's vast amount of user data to conduct espionage and surveillance operations against the U.S. Officials say China could covertly manipulate what U.S. TikTok users see on the site to spread disinformation and propaganda against American interests.

In 2021, the Biden administration gave ByteDance, TikTok's parent company, a chance to negotiate a security deal. ByteDance promised to wall off U.S. user data from Chinese access and let a U.S. government-approved partner, Texas-based Oracle, perform source-code reviews to guard against Chinese manipulation of its content-recommending algorithm. But its guarantee of a firewall failed to assuage U.S. officials.

Last spring, Congress received classified briefings about TikTok and passed the Protecting Americans from Foreign Adversary Controlled Applications Act with bipartisan support. The law, while known as the TikTok ban, didn't outright ban the app. To keep operating, ByteDance was given until Jan. 19 to divest itself of TikTok. The company said it can't and won't shed the platform. Instead, TikTok and a group of content creators challenged the ban in court in a pair of cases.

TikTok argues the ban violates the First Amendment right to free speech. How?

TikTok has tried to turn the case into a free-speech fight. It argues that its curation of content is an expressive activity protected by the First Amendment. In addition, the company says the U.S. government's security concerns are too vague and hypothetical to justify an infringement of its rights. TikTok has pointed to court cases supporting the right of Americans to share foreign propaganda. A unanimous federal appeals court upheld the ban last month, prompting TikTok to take its case to the Supreme Court.

If TikTok is banned on Jan. 19, what happens next?

The new law doesn't prohibit the use of TikTok, but it requires Apple and Google to remove TikTok from their app stores by that date. People who don't already have TikTok on their phone or tablet won't be able to download it from there. The law also bars U.S. internet hosting services and data-storage providers from supporting the app. Companies that defy the ban could face civil penalties that have the potential to reach billions of dollars.

While the social-media app won't suddenly vanish from Americans' phones and tablets, experts in legal compliance and mobile-app technology say TikTok could still be usable in the short term and envision it dying a slow death with progressively eroding functionality.

That is because TikTok is destined to degrade in quality once it leaves Apple and Google's app stores, as ByteDance won't be able to deploy software updates. Such updates are necessary for fixing bugs, patching missed security holes and taking into account any changes that Apple and Google make to their mobile operating systems.

Can I still get into TikTok if it is banned in the U.S.?

There are workarounds, but they are limited. One of the easiest is for U.S. users to access the TikTok website from their mobile devices via a web browser like Safari. But that experience provides only a stripped-down version of the video-sharing mobile app.

VPNs, or virtual private networks, are another potential option, since they can make it seem as if a user is in a country where TikTok isn't banned. Such services usually charge a small monthly fee for access to remote servers. VPNs let you route your traffic through one of those servers, preventing your internet service provider from seeing what apps you use, or any data you send and receive. Many VPNs for mobile devices are available via app stores.

If you have an Android device, you might be able to download TikTok — and even get software updates for it — by circumventing the Google Play Store, a tactic known as sideloading. This is a complex, multistep process and third-party app stores might not provide the protections against potential dangers such as malware.

Will President-elect Donald Trump save TikTok?

The last time Donald Trump was president, he tried and failed to shut down TikTok. The courts blocked his executive actions.

Now the president-elect has indicated he wants to come to the app's rescue. Trump, who asked the Supreme Court to delay the ban, says he is worried about its impact on TikTok users and the potential to set a dangerous precedent for internet regulation.

Trump says he needs more time to negotiate a resolution that would save the platform while addressing the security concerns. If the Supreme Court lets the ban take effect on schedule, Trump's options are limited. He could refuse to enforce the ban or push Congress to eliminate it through new legislation. Even with Trump's assurances, it would still be risky for Google and Apple to restore TikTok if the law is still on the books. The ban's broad support in Congress also tilts against a legislative way out.

Some legal scholars say the Trump administration could lift the ban without going through Congress if his administration determines the site is no longer under Chinese control.

Have other countries banned TikTok?

The U.S. would be the first Western liberal democracy to ban TikTok but not the first country. Around a dozen countries block the site, a list that includes India and authoritarian regimes such as Iran and Afghanistan that heavily censor the internet. In Europe, Albania's prime minister recently imposed a one-year shutdown of TikTok. It is also restricted in China, which has a TikTok-like site called Douyin that is subject to Chinese censorship laws. Numerous NATO and European Union countries have restrictions prohibiting TikTok on government work devices.

  • This explanatory article may be periodically updated.

Write to Jacob Gershman at jacob.gershman@wsj.com and Sarah E. Needleman at Sarah.Needleman@wsj.com

data.title: Renowned immunology investigator, Alice Long, joins Diamyd Medical's Scientific Advisory Board
data.description:
Diamyd Medical AB

Dr. Alice Long, a leading expert in translational immunology and diabetes research, has joined Diamyd Medical's Scientific Advisory Board (SAB). Dr. Long brings extensive experience in immunology and research aimed at understanding how individuals respond to immunotherapies.

"We are honored to welcome Dr. Long to Diamyd Medical's SAB," says Dr. Mark Atkinson, Chairman of Diamyd Medical's Scientific Advisory Board and Board member of Diamyd Medical. "Her landmark research around precision medicine and translation research for Type 1 Diabetes is fully aligned with the targeted approach of Diamyd®. Her support will be vital in shaping the scientific awareness and positioning our therapy."

Dr. Alice Long is an Associate Member and Principal Investigator at the Center for Translational Immunology at Benaroya Research Institute. Dr. Long's translational immunology lab investigates the mechanisms behind the loss of immune tolerance in autoimmune diseases and explores therapeutic strategies to restore it. Her research spans three interconnected areas: understanding the diminished IL-2 response in autoimmune patients, defining the function and stability of CD8 T cell exhaustion linked to favorable outcomes in autoimmunity, and uncovering factors that influence differential responses to therapy among patients.

"I am excited to join Diamyd Medical in its mission to transform care for individuals with Type 1 Diabetes," says Alice Long. "The company's focus on innovative therapies and precision medicine resonates deeply with my research goals. I look forward to collaborating with the team to advance groundbreaking treatments that improve patient outcomes and redefine the possibilities for managing autoimmune diseases."

About Diamyd Medical

Diamyd Medical develops precision medicine therapies to prevent and treat Type 1 Diabetes and LADA (Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults). Diamyd® is an antigen-specific immunomodulatory therapeutic for the preservation of endogenous insulin production. Diamyd® has been granted Orphan Drug Designation in the U.S. as well as Breakthrough Designation and Fast Track Designation by the U.S. FDA for the treatment of Stage 3 Type 1 Diabetes. Diamyd® has also been granted Fast Track Designation for the treatment of Stage 1 and 2 Type 1 Diabetes. DIAGNODE-3, a confirmatory Phase III trial is actively recruiting patients with recent-onset (Stage 3) Type 1 Diabetes at 60 clinics in eight European countries and in the US. Significant results have previously been shown in a large genetically predefined patient group - in a large-scale meta-analysis as well as in the Company's prospective European Phase IIb trial, where Diamyd® was administered directly into a superficial lymph node in children and young adults with recently diagnosed Type 1 Diabetes. Injections into a superficial lymph node can be performed in minutes and are intended to optimize the treatment response. A biomanufacturing facility is under development in Umeå, Sweden, for the manufacture of recombinant GAD65 protein, the active ingredient in the antigen-specific immunotherapy Diamyd®. Diamyd Medical also develops the GABA-based investigational drug Remygen® as a component in the treatments of metabolic diseases. Diamyd Medical is a major shareholder in the stem cell company NextCell Pharma AB and in the artificial intelligence company MainlyAI AB. Diamyd Medical's B share is traded on Nasdaq First North Growth Market under the ticker DMYD B. FNCA Sweden AB is the Company's Certified Adviser.

For further information, please contact:

Ulf Hannelius, President and CEO

Phone: +46 736 35 42 41

E-mail: ulf.hannelius@diamyd.com

Diamyd Medical AB (publ)

Box 7349, SE-103 90 Stockholm, Sweden. Phone: +46 8 661 00 26, Fax: +46 8 661 63 68

E-mail: info@diamyd.com Reg. no.: 556242-3797 Website: https://www.diamyd.com

The information was provided by the contact person above, for publication on January 9, 2025, 11:30 CET.

data.title: Inside the American Airlines Campaign to Win Back Business Travelers — WSJ
data.description:
By Alison Sider

American Airlines is trying to win back American corporations.

The carrier missed out on $1.5 billion in revenue last year in a failed bid to overhaul how it competes for business travel. After a monthslong mea culpa from American executives, the airline is restoring some perks, introducing deep discounts and rebuilding its sales ranks to win over what has been one of its most reliable and lucrative groups of customers.

The moves are critical for the nation's second largest carrier by passenger traffic to catch up with rivals that siphoned away corporate travelers. United Airlines and Delta Air Lines, which accounted for most of the industry's profits last year, both reported gains in business travel.

"We need to re-embrace that channel, win back that business," said Steve Johnson, the American Airlines executive given the job to oversee the airline's commercial organization.

American has reported progress from its business-customer charm offensive, but said it would take much of 2025 to recover lost ground. And some corporate travel buyers say that, while they appreciate the airline's efforts so far, American hasn't fully won them over.

"They are earnestly trying to fix the things they broke over the last couple years," said Bob Brindley, a consultant who helps corporate clients manage air and hotel suppliers. "Some of the issues will take a while to recover from."

The strategy

American announced its plan to overhaul business travel in 2022, aiming to cut out the middlemen it thought mucked up its relationship with fliers.

Airlines spend millions of dollars on commissions to travel agencies and discounts to corporate customers. But business travel remained sluggish long after leisure travel bounced back from Covid-19 pandemic. American thought its global network and loyalty program would be as strong a draw to travelers as the corporate deals. A sales force that once numbered in the "high hundreds" was reduced to fewer than 100 people.

American pulled some of its lowest fares from traditional corporate-booking channels to nudge travelers to book flights directly through the airline or newer platforms that link straight to it.

That strategy suited some road warriors. Jarrod West, who travels frequently for his work at a tech company, persuaded his bosses to let him go to American's website to book his work flights. He reasoned he would get better service with his premium status with the airline than through the company's travel manager.

But, to ensure compliance with corporate policies, many companies don't want employees going around their booking systems. Travel managers said they lost other important protections and benefits such as being able to change names on tickets.

Some buyers said the technological shift American was pushing is overdue. But the abrupt change to new systems created headaches at a time when the airline gutted its relationship with agencies, said David Richardson, executive vice president of Flight Centre Travel Group.

"The technology, from a corporate perspective, just was not ready," he said.

So the corporate travel industry fought back.

Defense contractor Lockheed Martin retracted American's status as a preferred carrier in its flight-booking platform. The carrier's flights appeared lower in search results. Travelers who tried to book flights with American often saw pop-up messages about alternatives. The company was able to slash its spending with American — once its most frequently used carrier — by 30%.

As corporate travel rebounded, the gap between American and its rivals began to widen.

American last year hired the consulting firm Bain to take the pulse of its corporate customers.

The companies had not only moved business away from the airline but also were angry.

"I think what was surprising was the extent of the unhappiness," Johnson said.

Changing course

American quickly pivoted, calling off a plan to restrict awarding frequent-flier miles to customers making bookings on its website and through certain agencies that used preferred distribution channels. The airline parted ways with Vasu Raja, the architect of the strategy, last spring. Raja declined to comment.

American Chief Executive Robert Isom has been on an apology tour, calling up or meeting with the CEOs of International Business Machines, Microsoft, Ernst & Young and dozens more of the airline's large corporate customers to hear their concerns. The airline in October hosted a customer forum at its headquarters.

American has brought two of its sales veterans back to consult and has been staffing up. It is planning to hire another 175 people to its sales team in the first half of 2025 — putting it back on par with where it was before.

The airline has been restoring perks it had stopped providing to corporate customers — benefits such as better boarding groups, better care during disruptions and access to preferred seats without an extra fee. It has also been redoing contracts with many of those customers.

"Whatever you ask for, they're being more flexible and open," said Colleen Kearney, associate director of global corporate travel at RTX.

At first, the conversations were tense, Johnson said. A few companies initially demanded to be reimbursed for losses they suffered as a result of American's moves — something American hasn't agreed to do.

While American's share of revenue from companies and agencies remains below historic levels, the airline says those bookings have been picking up. Isom said he expects to keep recapturing corporate market share over the course of this year.

Mark Stansbury, senior manager of global travel, events and logistics at Lockheed, said the airline has been moving in the right direction. "They're being very cooperative and open," he said.

Lockheed is no longer penalizing American in its systems, but some travelers who tried other airlines haven't switched back: "It's not as easy as turning on a faucet," Stansbury said.

Write to Alison Sider at alison.sider@wsj.com
